summary: Gruber examines the implications of foldable phones approaching the physical thickness limit imposed by USB-C ports (2.6mm minimum). He outlines four possible futures: phones never getting thinner than 4mm, going fully inductive/wireless, Apple creating a proprietary thinner port, or the USB Consortium creating a thinner standard. He concludes that option (b) — dropping physical ports in favor of MagSafe and wireless — is the most likely path, probably starting with a rumored iPhone 17 Air while Pro models retain USB-C longer. He notes the EU's charging port regulations add regulatory complexity to any proprietary solution.
key_points: Oppo's Find N5 foldable approaches ~4mm thickness, making the USB-C port (2.6mm minimum) the limiting factor for further thinness
The most likely path forward is dropping USB-C for inductive charging on consumer models while Pro models retain ports for high-speed data transfer
A proprietary thinner-than-USB-C port would be blocked by EU regulations, making it impractical to pursue
The EU mandate only applies to charging ports, so devices charging exclusively via inductive/magnetic connectors (like Apple Watch) are exempt
Credit-card-thin phones remain a distant goal requiring breakthroughs in battery, display, chip efficiency, and more
money_quotes: Option (a) strikes me as guaranteed wrong. We will, sooner or later, see phones that are too thin for a USB-C port. The only question is when.
Because of the ubiquity of USB, no one has even tried establishing a inductive connector for high-speed data transfer.
Option (d) makes me laugh just thinking about it.
My spidey-sense says this just isn't worth working on, and thus won't happen.

What I can imagine are phones and tablets that are more like credit cards in thickness. But there are so many engineering challenges that need to be solved to get there — battery technology, display technology, chip efficiency, and probably a dozen more.

I believe it would neatly remain within the EU's charging port regulations, but would irritate proponents of those regulations, because it's unlikely Apple would choose to make such a Super Smart Connector an open standard.
key_insight: The USB-C port is becoming the physical bottleneck for phone thinness, and the most likely resolution is Apple dropping wired ports entirely on consumer models while EU regulations ironically accelerate the move to proprietary wireless charging solutions.
